# The Terminal and File System {#sec-terminal}

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

- Open a terminal on macOS, Windows, and Linux, and identify the prompt and working directory.
- Navigate a file system using `pwd`, `ls`, and `cd`, and distinguish between relative and absolute paths.
- Create, move, copy, and inspect files from the command line.
- Diagnose `command not found` errors by reasoning about the system `PATH`.

## Why the Terminal Matters

Every graphical application you use — Jupyter Notebook, VS Code, Excel, your file manager — is a visual wrapper around operations that happen in the terminal. When you double-click a file to open it, your operating system is running a command. When you save a file, it is writing bytes to a location on disk. Learning to use the terminal directly gives you access to the same operations without the graphical middleman, which matters for three practical reasons.

First, many data tools — including [SQLite](https://www.sqlite.org/cli.html), [git](https://git-scm.com/docs), and package managers like [conda](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/stable/commands/index.html) — are designed to be used from the terminal. You will encounter them throughout this book and throughout your career. Second, terminal commands are scriptable: you can record a sequence of steps in a file and replay them exactly, which makes your workflow reproducible. Third, when something goes wrong in a graphical tool, the terminal is often the only way to diagnose the problem.

This chapter introduces the small set of terminal commands you will use repeatedly in this book. You do not need to memorize them — you need to understand what they do so you can look them up when you need them. If the terminal feels intimidating at first, that is completely normal. You are learning a new interface to your own computer, and the fluency comes with repetition.

## Opening a Terminal

On **macOS**, open the Terminal application (found in Applications → Utilities, or search for "Terminal" with Spotlight). On **Windows**, open Anaconda Prompt from the Start menu — this is a terminal preconfigured with access to your Python environment. You can also use PowerShell or Windows Terminal, though some commands differ. On **Linux**, open your distribution's terminal emulator.

When the terminal opens, you see a **prompt** — a line of text ending with `$` (Mac/Linux) or `>` (Windows) — followed by a blinking cursor. This is where you type commands. The terminal executes each command when you press Enter and prints any output below it.

## Where Am I? Navigating the File System

Your computer's file system is a tree of directories (folders) and files. At any moment, your terminal session is "inside" one directory, called the **working directory**. Every command you run operates relative to this location unless you specify an absolute path.

### Printing the working directory

To find out where you are, use `pwd` (print working directory):

```bash
pwd
```

On Mac or Linux, you might see something like `/Users/yourname` or `/home/yourname`. On Windows, the equivalent command is `cd` with no arguments, and the output looks like `C:\Users\yourname`.

### Listing files

To see what is in the current directory, use `ls` (list):

```bash
ls
```

On Windows, the equivalent is `dir`. To see more detail — file sizes, modification dates, and hidden files — use `ls -la` on Mac/Linux. The `-l` flag means "long format" (one file per line with details) and `-a` means "all files" (including hidden files whose names start with a dot, like `.gitignore`).

### Changing directories

To move into a subdirectory, use `cd` (change directory) followed by the folder name:

```bash
cd Documents
```

To move up one level (to the parent directory), use `cd ..`:

```bash
cd ..
```

To jump to your home directory from anywhere, use `cd ~` on Mac/Linux or `cd %USERPROFILE%` on Windows.

### Absolute vs. relative paths

A **relative path** starts from your current directory: `cd Documents/projects` moves into the `projects` folder inside `Documents`. An **absolute path** starts from the root of the file system: `/Users/yourname/Documents/projects` on Mac, or `C:\Users\yourname\Documents\projects` on Windows. Absolute paths work regardless of where your terminal session currently is.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Building a mental model
Think of `pwd` as asking "Where am I?", `ls` as asking "What's here?", and `cd` as saying "Go there." These three commands are the navigation triad you will use constantly.
:::

## Creating and Managing Files

### Creating directories

Use `mkdir` (make directory) to create a new folder:

```bash
mkdir my_project
```

To create nested directories in one step, add the `-p` flag:

```bash
mkdir -p my_project/data/raw   # -p creates all intermediate directories at once
                               # without -p, mkdir errors if a parent doesn't exist
```

### Moving and copying files

Use `cp` to copy a file and `mv` to move (or rename) a file:

```bash
cp original.csv backup.csv          # copy
mv old_name.csv new_name.csv        # rename
mv data.csv data/raw/data.csv       # move to a subdirectory
```

On Windows, the equivalents are `copy` and `move`.

### Viewing file contents

To see the first few lines of a text file without opening it in an editor, use `head`:

```bash
head -5 my_data.csv
```

This prints the first 5 lines — useful for checking whether a CSV has the header row you expect. On Windows, use `Get-Content my_data.csv -Head 5` in PowerShell, or `type my_data.csv | more` in Command Prompt.

## Paths and Your Python Environment

When you run a command like `python` or `sqlite3`, your terminal searches for that program in a list of directories called the **PATH**. If the program is not in any of those directories, you get a `command not found` error — even if the program is installed somewhere on your computer.

This is the most common source of confusion when setting up tools. The fix is usually one of two things: either install the program through a package manager that automatically updates your PATH (such as `conda install sqlite`), or manually add the program's directory to your PATH. The details vary by operating system, but the concept is the same everywhere. Managing Python environments — including configuring the PATH correctly — is covered in @sec-environments.

The directory you navigate to in your terminal is your **working directory** for any scripts or notebooks you run from that location. Organizing your project files consistently makes this easier to manage; see @sec-projects for the recommended directory structure.

::: {.callout-warning}
## The most common Windows mistake
After installing a new tool or editing your PATH, you must open a **new** terminal window. The old window will not see the change.
:::

## Common Issues in This Chapter

::: {.callout-warning}
## `command not found` or `'cd' is not recognized`
**What it means:** The shell cannot find the program you typed. Either you are on the wrong operating system's equivalent command (`ls` on Windows Command Prompt does not exist — use `dir`), the program is not installed, or it is installed but not on your `PATH`.

**How to fix it:** Confirm you are using the correct command for your OS. If a tool you installed is missing, close and reopen your terminal. If that does not work, re-read the install instructions — most tools print the `PATH` line you need to add at the end of their installer output.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## `No such file or directory`
**What it means:** The file or folder name you typed does not exist relative to your current working directory.

**How to fix it:** Run `pwd` (or `cd` with no arguments on Windows) to confirm where you are. Then run `ls` (or `dir`) to see what is actually in the folder. Spelling and capitalization matter — `Documents` and `documents` are different on Mac and Linux.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## `Permission denied`
**What it means:** Your user account does not have the rights to read, write, or execute the target. This is most common when you try to modify system folders or files created by another user.

**How to fix it:** Avoid using `sudo` reflexively — stay in your home directory (`~` on Mac/Linux, `%USERPROFILE%` on Windows) for your own projects. If you genuinely need to change a system file's permissions, learn what you are doing first; breaking system permissions can render your machine unbootable.
:::

## Key Takeaways

- The terminal is a text interface to the same operations your graphical tools perform. It is not advanced or scary — it is a different surface for actions you already know.
- `pwd`, `ls`, and `cd` are the navigation triad. Build reflex memory for these three commands before worrying about anything else.
- Paths can be **relative** (from where you are) or **absolute** (from the root of the file system). Know which you are using.
- `command not found` almost always means the program is not on your `PATH`, not that it is missing. Open a fresh terminal after any install.

## Further Reading

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## 📚 Going deeper on the terminal

- **The Missing Semester of Your CS Education (MIT)** — [missing.csail.mit.edu](https://missing.csail.mit.edu/) is an excellent free course on the terminal, shell scripting, and developer tooling.
- **Bash cheat sheet** — [devhints.io/bash](https://devhints.io/bash) is a compact reference for common shell commands.
- **Windows PowerShell basics** — [learn.microsoft.com/powershell](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/scripting/learn/ps101/01-getting-started) is the official starting guide.
- **SQLite command-line shell** — [sqlite.org/cli.html](https://www.sqlite.org/cli.html) documents `sqlite3` from the terminal, which you will use in @sec-sql-loading.
:::

## Exercises

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Exercise 1: Navigate to your project folder
Open a terminal and use `pwd`, `ls`, and `cd` to navigate to the folder where you keep your course materials. Create a new subdirectory called `data` inside it. Verify the directory was created by listing the contents of the parent folder. See @sec-projects for the recommended directory structure for course projects.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Exercise 2: Inspect a CSV from the terminal
Download one of the course datasets to your project folder. Use `head` (or the Windows equivalent) to view the first 10 lines. Based on what you see, answer: does the file have a single header row? What delimiter does it use? Are there any obvious problems visible in the first few rows?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Exercise 3: Exploring the PATH
Run `echo $PATH` in a terminal (Mac/Linux) or `echo %PATH%` (Windows). How many directories are listed? Then run `which python` (Mac/Linux) or `where python` (Windows) to find where your Python executable lives. Is that directory in your PATH? Close the terminal, open a new one, and run `echo $PATH` again — does the output match?

Explain in one sentence: what would happen if your Python installation directory were *not* in the PATH?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution
If Python's directory is not in the PATH, running `python` from the terminal gives a `command not found` error even though Python is installed. The operating system searches each directory in PATH in order and stops at the first match. If none of the directories contain the executable, the shell reports it as not found.
:::
:::
